request.question: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?

title: Clay County Savings Association Building
Edina Double Square Historic District
Bushwhacker
Clay County Courthouse (Alabama)
Claycomo, Missouri
Savings Bank Building
Old Clay County Courthouse (Clay, West Virginia)
Onondaga County Savings Bank
Clay County, Tennessee
Clay County Courthouse (Illinois)
sentences: The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.
The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.
The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.
Edina Double Square Historic District is a national historic district located at Edina, Knox County, Missouri.
The district encompasses 37 contributing buildings in the central business district of Edina.
It developed between about 1865 and 1945 and includes representative examples of Italianate and Streamline Moderne style architecture.

Notable contributing buildings include the Public Works Administration funded Knox County Courthouse (1934-1935) designed by William B. Ittner, Bishoff Bakery (1891), Northern Hotel (1860s), Ennis House/Northern Hotel (c. 1865), Edina School and Gymnasium (1915-1916), D. H. Mudd Building (c. 1904), Phillip Linville Building (c. 1908, c. 1963), Tobias J. Lycan Building (c. 1891), Jacob Pugh Building (c. 1891), Albert G. Bostick Building (c. 1891), Knox County Savings Bank (c. 1891), Thomas Burk Buildings (c. 1881), Bank of Edina Building (c. 1907), Joseph F. Biggerstaff Buildings (c. 1881), Stablein Building (c. 1870), and Knox County Public Library (c. 1915).

Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.
This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.
The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.
The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.
The Clay County Courthouse is a historic courthouse building in Ashland, Clay County, Alabama.
The Classical Revival-style building has served as the county courthouse since its completion in 1906.
Notable characteristics include its large dome, with clocks inset on four sides, and cupola surmounted by a statuary representing justice.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places on November 21, 1976.
Claycomo is a village in Clay County, Missouri, United States.
The village’s name is a portmanteau of "Clay, Co" (Clay County), and "Mo" (Missouri).
The population was 1,430 at the 2010 census.
Ford Motor Company has an assembly plant in Claycomo.
Kansas City Assembly is responsible for building the Ford F-150 pickup trucks and the Ford Transit vans.
The Savings Bank Building is a commercial building located at 101 South Front Street in Marquette, Michigan.
It is also known as the Marquette County Savings Bank.
The building was designated a Michigan State Historic Site in 1976 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.
The Old Clay County Courthouse in Clay, West Virginia was designed by Frank L. Packard and built in 1902.
The Beaux-Arts building was located on a hill overlooking the county seat.

The courthouse was the site of three notable trials: the Sarah Ann Legg trial of 1905, the first trial of a woman in Clay County for murder, the Booger Hole trial of 1917, in which citizens nearly lynched the defendants, and the Oscar Bail trial of 1953, in which Bail was convicted of killing a mine guard in the Great Widen Coal Strike.
The Onondaga County Savings Bank was chartered in 1855 in Syracuse, New York.
It was a franchise ahead of its time and had four separate branches by the late 19th century.
The "Onondaga County Savings Bank Building" was constructed in 1896 adjacent to the Erie Canal in "Clinton Square".
Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Tennessee.
As of the 2010 census, the population was 7,861.
Its county seat and only incorporated city is Celina.
Clay County is named in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.
Its current mayor is Dale Reagan.
The Clay County Courthouse, located at 300 Broadway Street in Louisville, is the county courthouse serving Clay County, Illinois.
Built in 1913, the courthouse was Clay County's fourth courthouse; it has served continuously as the seat of Clay County government since its opening.
Architect Joseph W. Royer, who planned several other Illinois courthouses, designed the Classical Revival building.
The courthouse was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?
hop1_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Aryan Republican Army | The Aryan Republican Army is the name given to white supremacist criminal group active in the United States in the early to mid-1990s.  The group is alleged to have associated with convicted bomber Timothy McVeigh in the months before the Oklahoma City bombing.  The organization was sometimes referenced in the media as the Midwest Bank Robbers.  The group was created by Peter Langan and Richard Lee Guthrie in 1992.  The group considered themselves a leaderless organization, meaning that there was no true leader to the group and that they were more of a group of associated people acting together to achieve their goals.  Although the group was mainly a criminal enterprise, they did have a terrorist like agenda.  With the money they were able to steal in the 22 confirmed robberies they were involved in, the group began stockpiling weapons and ammunition.  It is believed that the group wished to start a race war with the weapons that were stockpiled.  The group began to fall apart as members of the Aryan Republican Army were arrested after one of the former members of the group became an informant as part of a plea bargain.  By mid-1996, practically all known members had been apprehended and the group was in shambles.

State Line Mob | The State Line Mob was an association of criminal elements that operated in the 1950s and 1960s at the Mississippi–Tennessee state line in Alcorn County, Mississippi, and McNairy County, Tennessee, along U.S. Route 45.  The State Line Mob was involved in bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, tourist fleecing, robbery, and murder.  A few of the members were from Phenix City, Alabama, having been displaced from that town when martial law was declared by the Governor and the Alabama National Guard attempted to clean the town up.

Florencia 13 | The Florencia 13 is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Hispanic-Americans and some blacks.  The gangs name is after the Florence area of Los Angeles county, the 13 is for it's allegiance to the Mexican Mafia.  They are involved in drug smuggling, murder, assault, robbery etc.

Arifs (gang) | The Arifs are a South East London-based Turkish Cypriot criminal organization heavily involved in armed robbery, contract killing, drug trafficking and other racketeering-related activities within London's underworld since the late 1960s.  Following the downfall of the Kray brothers, the Arifs were one of several criminal organizations who took control of the London underworld including the Clerkenwell crime syndicate and the Brindle family with whom they were engaged in a highly publicized gangland war during the 1990s.

Quaker Springs, New York | Quaker Springs is a hamlet in Saratoga County.  The zip code is 12871.  Quaker Springs is between NY-32 Route 4.  Quaker Springs United Methodist Church is located in the hamlet.  An anti-slavery society was based in Quaker Springs and several possible participants in the Underground Railroad were in Quaker Springs.  The name Quaker Springs came from a type of mineral spring located in the area.  The area including Quaker Springs was involved in Revolutionary War positions.
U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II | U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
hop1_titles: U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
response.summary_1: Passage [1] reports the Clay County Savings Association Building "was robbed on February 13, 1866" and that the robbers were "a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang." The provided passages do not state the Revolutionary War–derived label referenced in the question; next-hop retrieval should look for sources linking these "former Confederate guerrillas" / the "James–Younger Gang" to the Revolutionary War term (search for e.g. "bushwhackers" or "<Revolutionary War term> + James–Younger Gang" or "former Confederate guerrillas named after Revolutionary War term").
response.hop2_query: James-Younger Gang bushwhackers Clay County Savings Association robbery
hop2_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

The Great Northfield Minnesota Raid | The Great Northfield Minnesota Raid is a 1972 Technicolor Western film about the James-Younger Gang distributed by Universal Pictures.  It was directed by Philip Kaufman in a cinéma vérité style and starred Cliff Robertson as Cole Younger, Robert Duvall as Jesse James, Luke Askew as Jim Younger, R. G. Armstrong as Clell Miller, John Pearce as Frank James, and Matt Clark as Bob Younger.  The film purports to recreate the James-Younger Gang's most infamous escapade, the September 7, 1876, robbery of "the biggest bank west of the Mississippi" in Northfield, Minnesota.

Clell Miller | Clell Miller (1849 or 1850 - September 7, 1876) (also known as Cleland D. Miller or Clenand Miller or McClelland Miller) was an outlaw with the James-Younger Gang who was killed during the gang's robbery at Northfield, Minnesota.

Newton Gang | The Newton Gang (ca. 1919 through 1924) was an outlaw gang of the early 20th century, and the most successful train robbers and bank robbers in history.  From 1919 through 1924 the gang robbed dozens of banks, claiming a number of eighty-seven banks (unconfirmed) and six trains (confirmed).  According to Willis Newton, the brothers "took in more money than the Dalton Gang, Butch Cassidy's Wild Bunch and the James-Younger Gang combined."  Also according to their own claims, they never killed anyone.  It's true they were never charged with any death or injuries associated with their robberies, although one daylight robbery in Toronto, Ontario, Canada proved nearly fatal for one bank messenger.  Notable enough for the 1924 train robbery near Rondout, Illinois (the world's largest at the time), the brothers gained a second round of fame in retirement, when they participated in a 1975 documentary film, and then a more in-depth oral history project that eventually was published in book form, possibly one of the clearest records of a criminal career of the period, as told by the participants.  This second round of fame led to a feature film being produced by a major Hollywood studio, after the death of the last surviving brother.

Johnny Hope | John "Johnny" Hope (1856–1906) was a 19th-century American burglar, bank robber and pickpocket.  The son of James "Old Jimmy" Hope, he was alleged to have been associated with his father and the George Leslie Gang.  He was among those arrested for the 1878 robbery of the Manhattan Savings Institution although he and Billy Kelly were the only men actually sent to prison for this crime.

Jesse James | Jesse Woodson James (September 5, 1847April 3, 1882) was an American outlaw, bank and train robber, guerrilla, and leader of the James–Younger Gang.  Raised in the "Little Dixie" area of western Missouri, James and his family maintained strong Southern sympathies.  He and his brother Frank James joined pro-Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers" operating in Missouri and Kansas during the American Civil War.  As followers of William Quantrill and "Bloody Bill" Anderson, they were accused of participating in atrocities against Union soldiers and civilian abolitionists, including the Centralia Massacre in 1864.

Wood Hite | Robert Woodson "Wood" Hite (1850 – December 4, 1881) was an outlaw and cousin of Frank and Jesse James.  He was a member of the James-Younger gang, participating in a number of robberies and other crimes.  He was shot dead by Robert Ford during a gunfight with Ford's friend Dick Liddil.  The death of Hite precipitated the series of events that culminated in the killing of Jesse James by Ford.
response.summary_2: The robbers were former Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers." Passage [6] corroborates that Jesse James and his brother joined pro-Confederate guerrillas called "bushwhackers"; the passages do not explicitly confirm a Revolutionary War derivation for the term.
